serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of stir-fried beef with mixed noodles or rice as typically served at a Singaporean hawker centre or coffee shop, approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-Fried Beef With Mixed Noodles Or Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Stir-Fried Beef With Mixed Noodles Or Rice' (cr_id G00010079). All per-100g values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed noodle and rice dishes analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for stir-fried beef with mixed noodles or rice. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database values for mixed noodle dishes and white rice (GI range 50–65). The dish contains both noodles and rice as the carbohydrate base; a blended midpoint GI of 56 is used. Dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100g and protein at 6.5 g/100g are expected to moderately attenuate the glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 88.2) / 100 ≈ 49.4, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 25.19 × 3.5 = 88.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; typical 350 g serving delivers approximately 809 mg sodium, representing 35–40% of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce, choose a smaller portion, and note that the high potassium content (327 mg/100g) partially offsets the sodium load per DASH diet principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content of 25.19 g/100g with a medium estimated GI of 56; a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 88 g of carbohydrate, which is a substantial glycaemic load for a single meal.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request brown rice or wholegrain noodles to lower the GI, keep the portion to 200–250 g, and note that dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g) and protein (6.5 g/100g) help moderate postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beef is a red meat associated with elevated saturated fat and haem iron intake; sodium at 231 mg/100g from soy and oyster sauces adds cardiovascular risk at larger serving sizes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 200 g, request less sauce to reduce sodium, and consider substituting beef with lean chicken or tofu to reduce saturated fat and haem iron exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which drives TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways central to RA pathology.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should replace beef with salmon, tofu, or chicken to reduce arachidonic acid load, and request reduced soy and oyster sauce to minimise sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Beef With Mixed Noodles Or Rice is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of thinly sliced beef stir-fried at high heat with a combination of noodles or steamed rice, mixed vegetables, soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, and cooking oil. The dish is a staple at Chinese-Singaporean coffee shops and hawker centres, typically served as a one-dish meal for lunch or dinner. The texture is a combination of tender beef, soft noodles or fluffy rice, and lightly cooked vegetables, with a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile from the soy and oyster sauces. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with a notable dietary fibre content of 3.7 g and a meaningful iron contribution of 2.55 mg. Sodium is moderate at 231 mg/100g, primarily from the sauces used. The dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions but requires caution for individuals managing hypertension (due to sodium from sauces), Type 2 Diabetes (due to the medium-GI carbohydrate base), and cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis (due to the red meat component). Requesting wholegrain noodles or brown rice, reduced sauce, and a moderate portion size significantly improves the nutritional profile for health-conscious consumers.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010079) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct entry exists for this specific dish.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010079) as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo G00010079); this is plausible for a dish where the beef quantity per 100g of the composite dish is moderate and the cholesterol may be below the reporting threshold. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for mixed noodle and rice dishes; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. Halal status is not confirmed as beef sourcing and sauce ingredients (oyster sauce) require halal certification verification.
